Donald Trump says Iran could be 'taken out in one night' if deal not reached by looming deadline

ShareShare article

# Donald Trump says Iran could be 'taken out in one night' if deal not reached by looming deadline

By [Brad Ryan](https://www.abc.net.au/news/brad-ryan/6017770) in Washington DC with wires

- [Topic:World Politics](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/world-politics)

2h ago2 hours agoMon 6 Apr 2026 at 5:14pm

Play video.

Has Video Duration: 40 seconds.

Watch 40s

![Trump speaking.l ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/13119661d05ec8e787759a6abcaef43b?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1920&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

Donald Trump threatened Iran with the "complete demolition" of its infrastructure.

## In short:

Donald Trump has reiterated threats to devastate Iran's civilian infrastructure if a deal is not reached soon.

The US president says any agreement must include reopening the Strait of Hormuz.

## What's next?

Mr Trump has given a deadline of 8pm Tuesday, Washington time (10am Wednesday, AEST) for a deal to be struck.

US President Donald Trump says the "entire country" of Iran will be "taken out" if no deal is reached by 10am Wednesday (AEST), ratcheting up his threat to target civilian infrastructure and brushing off concerns it would be a war crime.

In a lengthy White House press conference — in which he again criticised Australia and other allies — Mr Trump said the US had a plan to bomb every bridge and power plant in Iran.

"The entire country could be taken out in one night," Mr Trump said.

"And that night might be tomorrow night."

His comments came after Iran rejected a 45-day ceasefire proposal, which was raised in talks involving Egyptian, Pakistani and Turkish mediators. Mr Trump said Iran had put forward its own proposal, but it was "not good enough".

"We have a plan, because of the power of our military, where every bridge in Iran will be decimated by 12 o'clock tomorrow night, where every power plant in Iran will be out of business, burning, exploding, and never to be used again.

"I mean complete demolition," Mr Trump said. "And it will happen over a period of four hours if we want it to.

"We don't want that to happen. We may even get involved with helping them rebuild their nation."

In response, Iranian military spokesman Ebrahim Zolfaghari said: "If attacks on civilian targets are repeated, the subsequent phases of our offensive and retaliatory operations will be executed much more forcefully and extensively."

Earlier, at an Easter event, Mr Trump said negotiations with Iran were continuing.

![Melania and Donald Trump stand next to someone dressed as the Easter Bunny.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/3a77a74aa93b83a3b658e41ec79d190e?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Donald Trump discussed the war during multiple public appearances on Easter Monday. (AP Photo/Alex Brandon)

"They made a proposal, and it's a significant proposal. It's a significant step. It's not good enough."

The head of Iran's diplomatic mission in Cairo, Mojtaba Ferdousi Pour, told the Associated Press: "We only accept an end of the war with guarantees that we won't be attacked again."

He said Iran no longer trusted the Trump administration after the US bombed the Islamic Republic twice during previous rounds of talks.

## 'They want us to keep bombing'

Mr Trump used his press conference to once again criticise US allies that had not joined the war.

He complained NATO had "actually gone out of their way not to help".

> "You know who else didn't help us? Australia didn't help us,"

he said.

He similarly called out Japan and South Korea.

Play video.

Has Video Duration: 29 seconds.

Watch 29s

![Trump speaking. ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/36930ab4f26e1e93a7eba81826213dd8?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1920&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

Donald Trump has criticised Australia as well as allies in Europe and Asia.

He said securing the free flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz was now a "very big priority" for the US. That represents a turnaround from [recent comments in which he said the strait's closure was not America's problem](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-01/trump-anger-at-allies-as-hegseth-visits-mideast/106519152) and other countries would need to get it reopened.

He said any deal with Iran would need to include "the free traffic of oil and everything else".

When a reporter raised the point that targeting civilian infrastructure was a violation of international law, Mr Trump ignored the question and attacked the reporter's outlet, the New York Times.

He insisted the Iranian people "would be willing to suffer that in order to have freedom".

"They want us to keep bombing," he said.

At the Easter event earlier, Mr Trump said he wanted to "take the oil" from Iran, but "unfortunately the American people would like to see us come home".

"If it were up to me, I'd take the oil, I'd keep the oil," he said.

"I would make plenty of money. And I'd also take care of the people of Iran much better than they've been taken care of."

## 'Needle in a haystack' rescue mission

Mr Trump called the press conference to discuss last week's rescue of two air force members, including one who was missing in Iran after a fighter jet was shot down "deep inside enemy territory".

"I immediately was asked to make a decision," Mr Trump said. "I ordered the US Armed Forces to do whatever was necessary to bring our brave warriors back home. A risky decision, because we could have ended up with 100 dead as opposed to one or two."

Confirming many [previously reported details](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-06/how-us-forces-rescued-stranded-airman-in-iran-explainer/106533914), Mr Trump said one crew member was found relatively quickly and rescued by chopper "as their warriors faced gunfire at very close range".

The other climbed "treacherous mountain terrain" and hid in crevices to evade capture, Mr Trump said.

"He scaled cliff faces bleeding rather profusely, treated his own wounds, and contacted American forces to transmit his location."

Finding the missing crew member was "like finding a needle in a haystack", Mr Trump said. CIA director John Ratcliffe said it required the use of "exquisite technologies" that no other intelligence agency had, but he could not reveal details.

The rescue mission involved 155 aircraft, including four bombers, 64 fighters, 48 refuelling tankers and 13 rescue aircraft, Mr Trump said.

"A lot of it was subterfuge," he said. "We wanted to have them \[Iran\] think he was in a different location because they had a vast military force out there. Thousands and thousands of people were looking, so we wanted them to look in different areas."

The job was made more difficult after the media was told about the rescue, Mr Trump said. "We're looking very hard to find that leaker," he said, and vowed to force a reporter to reveal their source or go to jail.

He also said some military personnel opposed the rescue because it was so risky.

**ABC/AP**

Chinese AI satellite intelligence helping Iran target US forces with 'incredible precision', analysts say

# Chinese AI satellite intelligence helping Iran target US forces with 'incredible precision', analysts say

Exclusive by [Henry Zwartz](https://www.abc.net.au/news/henry-zwartz/8847638)

- [Topic:War](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/war)

6h ago6 hours agoMon 6 Apr 2026 at 4:03am

![A satellite image of a desert area with various military assets in it.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/30253e34d2afc4a2c9eb252abf4d174d?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=723&cropW=1286&xPos=21&yPos=37&width=862&height=485)

MizarVision published images of the deployment of US air defence systems in the Middle East in the lead-up to the Iran war and after it began. (Supplied: MizarVision)

## In short:

Analysts say images taken by a Chinese company could be helping Iran to target its opponents with extreme precision.

A retired Australian major general says the capability could put Australian troops at risk.

The company behind the satellite images has not responded to the ABC's requests for comment and a US analyst says the Chinese government should intervene.

Chinese satellite images enhanced by artificial intelligence could help Iran target US and allied forces to within a third of a square metre, military analysts say.

The ABC has revealed the US Defense Intelligence Agency (DIA) has assessed that an AI tool used by a Chinese company MizarVision [poses a threat to US forces](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-06/us-intelligence-chinese-satellite-imagery-middle-east/106508322).

The technology identifies and tags military bases and other operations across vast areas.

Retired Australian Defence Force Major General Gus McLachlan said militaries would need to factor in Iran's ability to potentially target sites as small as 0.3 square metres.

"We're seeing targets, including a US E-3 Sentry plane, being hit with incredible precision. Our forces would take this threat very seriously — and rightfully so," he said.

"The Australian government would be concerned. It's a plausible outcome that Australians could be killed by this capability.

"This is a dangerous development because it empowers the Iranians to choose exactly what to target. Any object left in a spot for 24 hours could be vulnerable, now, to an Iranian targeting cycle."

About 100 Australian soldiers are stationed in the Middle East, including at [facilities damaged in an Iranian attack in the United Arab Emirates](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-18/al-minhad-air-base-attacked-by-iran-no-australians-hurt/106468378) on March 18.

American military analyst Ryan Fedasiuk, a public service fellow with the US Department of State, said AI-enhanced satellite imagery created by MizarVision could enable Iranian forces to target US troops and bases in close to real time.

"Australian and US soldiers in the Middle East could be killed because of this. This is real-time battlefield information that is being used to harm US soldiers. It's remarkable," he said, reacting to the ABC story.

MizarVision has been using the software to identify different military capabilities, including specific types of planes, the location of navy ships, and the deployment of air defence systems across the Middle East.

Such capability once required the resources of a national intelligence agency.

Mr Fedasiuk said Iran did not have the ability to tag US military assets in near-real time before the war.

"Iran doesn't have robust access to satellite capability. This provides them with that capability," he said.

Mr Fedasiuk has authored papers on the military use of AI, including within the People's Liberation Army of China.

According to business listings, MizarVision is a private company that was founded in 2021 and has 5.5 per cent government ownership.

MizarVision's website states the company's mission is to lower "the barriers to geospatial intelligence discovery and analysis … making it no longer the exclusive capability of a few organisations".

![A satellite image showing military planes in rows in a desert area.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/b346080627bb51e3ebd4110029bd2a78?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=809&cropW=1213&xPos=61&yPos=19&width=862&height=575)

This image of Prince Sultan Air Base was published on February 25. (Supplied: MizarVision)

## Iran outsourcing military intel

Neither Mr Fedasiuk nor Major General McLachlan have seen the classified DIA assessment.

Reacting to the ABC report, Mr Fedasiuk said Iran was effectively outsourcing targeting data from Chinese enterprises.

"This is a further escalation to provide aid, quite directly, to Iranian forces. Perhaps the greatest degree of support by China for a proxy force against the United States in the war," he said.

"It's a significant development. The company has zeroed in on US forces and it's valuable information that is useful to the IRGC \[Islamic Revolutionary Guard\]."

In a statement to the ABC, China's Ministry of Foreign Affairs said reports that a Chinese company was helping Iranian forces were "sensationalist".

"It is understood that the satellite imagery released by the relevant companies was obtained from open-source channels and is a routine market practice," it told the ABC.

The ministry said Chinese companies were always required "to conduct their business in accordance with laws and regulations".

Mr Fedasiuk said the AI capability was once confined to just a few countries and that China had a responsibility to intervene.

"This is now in the public domain. China has an obligation to step in and stop this," he said.

"While there are legitimate questions to be asked about the degree of Chinese government support, it's hard to believe China's government can't control it — although it could be that China is not aware of the extent of the value this intelligence provides to the IRGC."

## Satellite providers go dark over security risks

Over the weekend satellite company Planet Labs said the US government had asked all satellite imagery providers to indefinitely withhold images of the conflict region.

"There are genuine concerns of use of Planet Labs data over Iran, as well as an extended window of risk for recent imagery," a company spokesperson told the ABC before that announcement.

The restriction expanded a 14-day delay on imagery of the Middle East that Planet Labs implemented last month.

The US-based company said the move was meant to prevent adversaries from using the imagery to attack the US and its allies.

MizarVision has not responded to repeated requests for comment.

_Major General McLachlan has a financial interest in the Australian company DroneShield._

# Australia's fuel shipments secured 'well into' May, energy minister says

By political reporter [Maani Truu](https://www.abc.net.au/news/maani-truu/100273642)

- [Topic:Petrol Prices](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/petrol-prices)

2h ago2 hours agoSun 5 Apr 2026 at 11:00pm

![A close up of Chris Bowen, looking serious and wearing a suit and tie.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/00d8dbd817a8dfc7a73e6b597680c7f5?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=720&cropW=1280&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

Chris Bowen says fuel shipments continue to arrive as expected. (ABC News: David Sciasci)

## In short:

Energy Minister Chris Bowen says fuel shipments to Australia are now secured "well into" May, as the war in the Middle East continues with no end in sight.

The number of service stations out of diesel and petrol fell over the Easter weekend, which Mr Bowen said was encouraging.

## What's next?

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has warned Australians that the coming months "may not be easy", as the global oil crisis continues to drive up fuel demand and prices domestically.

Fuel shipments to Australia have been secured "well into" May and the number of service stations with diesel and petrol shortages have fallen over the Easter weekend, Energy Minister Chris Bowen has said.

The government previously said Australia's fuel supplies were locked in until the end of April, but Mr Bowen told reporters today that authorities were now working to secure supplies for the following month.

"All the orders are locked in and contracted. Once it's contracted, the fuel belongs to the Australian company that's bought it … that is legally locked in, so that's encouraging," he told ABC RN.

"Of course, there is a risk in international circumstance and \[the\] international situation, but every step that can be taken is being taken."

According to the latest data, 274 service stations across the country are now out of diesel — down from more than 400on Good Friday.

In New South Wales, the state with the highest number of shortages, 6 per cent of stations were now out of diesel, Mr Bowen said.

"Most of the other outages are around 2 to 3 per cent around the country. New South Wales has been higher because the farmers are seeding and sowing and they've been the priority, to get fuel to them," he said.

"That means we've had more outages in New South Wales than anyone would like, but that figure has been coming down, including over Easter."

| **State or territory** | Service stations without fuel |
| --- | --- |
| **New South Wales** | 142 without diesel, 39 with no fuel. |
| **Victoria** | 51 without diesel, 30 with no unleaded. |
| **Queensland** | 38 without diesel, 32 with no unleaded. |
| **South Australia** | 9 without diesel, 5 with no unleaded. |
| **Western Australia** | 19 without diesel, 29 with no unleaded. |
| **Tasmania** | 7 without diesel, 7 with no unleaded. |
| **Northern Territory** | 4 without diesel, 4 with no unleaded. |
| **Australian Capital Territory** | 4 without diesel, 2 with no unleaded. |

On Friday, Mr Bowen said [53 ships carrying fuel](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-03/chris-bowen-fuel-update-industry-concerned-not-enough/106530238) were on the way to Australia from refineries around the world, including in Asia, the United States and Mexico.

Australia and Singapore have signed a joint commitment to continue the trade of fuels, with the government seeking to land similar deals with other Asian nations.

"The prime minister's been, of course, talking to his counterparts. I've been talking to mine. Foreign Minister \[Penny\] Wong has been talking to hers," Mr Bowen told reporters at a media conference in Sydney.

"We continue to see no ships cancelled that have been contracted for Australia, \[and\] extra orders being delivered."

The country's fuel stockpile remains at 39 days of petrol, 29 days of diesel and 30 days of jet fuel.

Mr Bowen said this had been "pretty consistent" since the beginning of the war.

Mr Albanese used a [rare address to the nation last week](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-01/albanese-reassures-public-address-to-the-nation-amid-fuel-crisis/106521200) to ask Australians to switch to public transport if they could in an effort to save fuel, but simultaneously stressed that people should continue to live as normal as the crisis rolled on.

But he also acknowledged that the coming months "may not be easy", as the war in the Middle East continued without a clear end in sight.

"These are uncertain times, but I am absolutely certain of this: we will deal with these challenges in the Australian way, working together and looking after each other, as we always have," Mr Albanese said.

# Train splits Tour of Flanders as Tadej Pogačar wins for third time, Demi Vollering wins women's race

- [Topic:Cycle Sport](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/cycle-sport)

38m ago38 minutes agoSun 5 Apr 2026 at 8:41pm

![Cyclists wait with police at a level crossing during the Tour of Flanders race.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/6724be38db20362eb363f0b2fb7ea7e6?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2813&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=521&width=862&height=485)

Riders were held at the level crossing before being allowed to catch up to the leaders. (Getty Images: Dario Belingheri)

## In short:

Tadej Pogačar has won the one-day Tour of Flanders for a record-equalling third time after the men's field was split by a train passing through a level crossing.

Race officials told the lead group of about 20 to allow the peloton to catch up, leading to an enthralling battle between Pogačar and rival Mathieu van der Poel.

Riders have been disqualified in the past for going through red lights at a level crossing, and there are reports that the offending riders could be fined by authorities.

Cycling great Tadej Pogačar has added another prestigious victory by winning the Tour of Flanders for a record-equalling third time after a train crossing split the field in the one-day classic race.

Slovenia's Pogačar made it through as the boom gates came down at a level crossing and completed a dominant solo effort against a world-class field, further cementing his place in cycling lore as one of eight three-time winners in the race's 113-year history.

Also known as De Ronde, the Belgian race is one of the "monuments" of cycling — the five most prestigious one-day events in the sport — along with Milan-San Remo, Paris-Roubaix, Liege-Bastogne-Liege and the Giro di Lombardia.

Pogačar, from the UAE Team Emirates-XRG team, and his great one-day classic rival Mathieu van der Poel (Alpecin-Premier Tech) were the strongest in the pack, although van der Poel and fellow contender Wout van Aert were among those stopped around the 212-kilometre mark by a passing train while Pogačar and Remco Evenepoel carried on in a lead group of about 20.

Twitter Embed

Copy link

[Read 6 replies](https://twitter.com/cyclingontnt/status/2040740514635399355?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw%7Ctwcamp%5Etweetembed%7Ctwterm%5E2040740514635399355%7Ctwgr%5Ec28c626941c7192680ed980f93fb533f77b4aa85%7Ctwcon%5Es1_&ref_url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.abc.net.au%2Fnews%2F2026-04-06%2Ftrain-splits-tour-of-flanders-as-tadej-pogacar-wins-again%2F106534156)

Race officials told the leading group to slow down to allow the peloton to catch up, but the incident raised questions over UCI rules as it appeared the leaders ignored red lights, which could lead to disqualification.

In a similar incident at the 2006 Paris-Roubaix, Leif Hoste, Peter Van Petegem and Vladimir Gusev were disqualified and stripped of their second-, third- and fourth-placed finishes.

The European Broadcasting Union reported railway operator Infrabel criticised the cyclists' actions while the East Flanders Public Prosecution Service confirmed plans to legally prosecute the riders, citing the case as a severe traffic violation, which could attract thousands of euros' worth of fines.

![Cyclists in the Tour of Flanders wait for a train to pass.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/40c272352dece8eec4f053b5577b62cf?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The peloton stopped due to a train crossing the route about three-quarters of the way through the race. (Getty Images: Dario Belingheri)

After the field regrouped, van der Poel rode with Pogačar at the front and they dropped all other contenders with about 18 kilometres remaining in the gruelling 278-kilometre route, before [world champion Pogačar](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-09-29/pogacar-pedals-to-another-epic-world-cycling-title/105829612) launched an attack in the final ascent of the Oude Kwaremont.

Pogačar's move last year on the same climb had proved decisive, but this time van der Poel was not immediately dropped and managed to limit the gap to just a few seconds at the top of the hill.

Van der Poel, however, could not close the gap and Pogačar gradually extended his lead, redoubling his effort on the Paterberg, a short but brutal cobbled climb with gradients exceeding 20 per cent.

That proved too much for van der Poel, himself a three-time Ronde winner, who lagged by 15 seconds at the summit and ultimately crossed the line 34 seconds behind [four-time Tour de France winner Pogačar](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-07-28/tadej-pogacar-wins-fourth-tour-de-france/105580146).

![Demi Vollering and Tadej Pogačar with their Tour of Flanders trophies.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/ba68c1ecbd568fefb49aaad064093837?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Demi Vollering (left) won the women's race and Tadej Pogačar took the men's title for the third time. (Getty Images: Billy Ceusters)

"It was really a crazy race today," Pogačar said.

"It was super hard and then a bit of a waiting game."

On his Tour of Flanders debut, Evenepoel (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe) finished third — a minute and 11 seconds off the pace — ahead of van Aert (Visma-Lease a Bike) a further 53 seconds back.

FDJ Suez rider Demi Vollering won the women's race for the first time.

Like Pogačar, she attacked in the Oude Kwaremont and finished 42 seconds ahead of Tour de France champion Pauline Ferrand-Prévot, with Puck Pieterse completing the podium.

Ruby Roseman-Gannon was the best-placed Australian, crossing in a group with Liv AlUla Jayco teammate Mackenzie Coupland 6:53 back in 23rd and 36th.

Five other Australian women did not finish.

In the men's race, Luke Durbridge crossed in 99th, 11:38 behind Pogačar, along with teammate and compatriot Kelland O'Brien. Sam Welsford and Jarrad Drizners did not finish.

How US forces pulled off daring rescue of stranded airman shot down in Iran

ShareShare article

# How US forces pulled off daring rescue of stranded airman shot down in Iran

[Rudi Maxwell](https://www.abc.net.au/news/rudi-maxwell/105702270) with wires

- [Topic:Unrest, Conflict and War](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/unrest-conflict-and-war)

19h ago19 hours agoMon 6 Apr 2026 at 12:12am

Play video.

Has Video Duration: 37 seconds.

Watch 37s

![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/04b8de1c6d8b357af5d611a8dbff8456?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=810&cropW=1440&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

Video captures the wreckage of a US plane and helicopter in Iran.

The US military has pulled off a daring rescue of two aviators whose jet fighter was shot down by Iran.

American forces plucked the pilot from behind enemy lines before a complicated extraction of the second airman, who hid in a mountain crevice as Tehran called for Iranians to help capture him.

When Iranian fire hit a US F-15E Strike Eagle jet fighter on Friday the two-man crew ejected just in time, landing behind enemy lines.

The pilot was rescued in a matter of hours after US military forces were able to verify his position and send in a rescue mission.

But the jet fighter's weapons specialist was missing, and the effort to rescue him was anything but straightforward, involving the CIA, subterfuge and specialist aircraft in a second mission after the first ran into difficulty deep in hostile territory.

A US official said the plane was flying over Isfahan province when it was brought down and the two airmen ejected separately.

Even as US President Donald Trump and other US officials described an almost cinematic mission, rescuers faced major obstacles, including two Black Hawk helicopters coming under fire and problems with two transport planes that forced the US military to blow them up.

## How did the rescue mission begin?

The rescue began with near‑perfect precision.

Under the cover of darkness, US commandos slipped deep into Iran, undetected, scaled a 2,100 metre ridge and pulled the stranded American weapons specialist to safety, moving him toward a secret rendezvous point before dawn on Sunday.

Then everything stopped.

Two C-130 transport aircraft that had ferried some of the roughly 100 special operations forces into rugged terrain south of Tehran suffered a mechanical failure and could not take off, a US official said, speaking on condition of anonymity.

Suddenly, elite commandos risked being stuck behind enemy lines.

![A close-up of a US Airforce Airman wearing a helmet and looking through a site at night.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/90c3a6c12358a833404c476fe6643fc1?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The US Air Force sent troops on a C-130J Super Hercules aircraft to rescue a stranded officer in Iran. (Supplied: US Air Force)

Their commanders made a high-risk decision, ordering additional aircraft to fly into Iran to extract the group in waves — a decision that left the elite commandos waiting for a couple of tense hours.

"If there was a 'holy shit' moment, that was it," said the official, who credited quick decision-making with saving the day.

The gamble worked.

The rescue force was pulled out in stages, and US troops destroyed the disabled C‑130s and four additional helicopters inside Iran rather than risk leaving sensitive equipment behind.

The extraction operation launched overnight on Saturday, local time,  involved hundreds of special operations troops, including Navy SEAL Team 6 commandos, US media reports said.

The navy commandos were tasked with extracting the airman, while US attack aircraft provided cover, the New York Times added, citing unnamed officials.

## How did the airman survive?

US aviators undergo Survival, Evasion, Resistance and Escape (SERE) training in the event they have to land in hostile territory, but few are fluent in Persian and face a challenge in staying undetected while seeking rescue.

Their combat vests contain a radio/GPS-coded beacon to transmit their position, a communication device, as well as water, food, first-aid material and a pistol.

![A soldier inspects a survival vest laid out on a table. He is in khakis. Other equipment behind.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/7ed3d3d5454ea9c4ed4d9e78efeb40ae?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2789&cropW=4184&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

US Air Force personnel are equipped with survival vests with water pouches, signalling devices and communication aids. (US Air Force: Erin Dunkleberger)

The airman was wounded after his ejection but could still walk, reportedly scaling a 2,100-metre ridge in the mountains before hiding in a crevice, according to the New York Times and Axios.

A US source familiar with some of the operational details said the American officer sprained his ankle.

The official said the airman later established contact with the US military and authenticated himself — a critical step to ensure rescue forces were not walking into a trap.

## Why didn't Iranian forces find the stranded officer?

The CIA ran a deception campaign, hoping to confuse Tehran by planting information inside Iran that US forces had already located the missing airman and were moving him before the operation took place, a senior Trump administration official said.

And the US military took additional steps, jamming electronics and bombing key roads around the location to prevent people from getting close, a US source familiar with the planning said.

The source said that the aircraft eventually sent to extract the airman and rescue forces were much smaller turboprop aircraft, capable of landing on small airfields and relatively light.

Iranian authorities had encouraged local people to join security forces searching for the airman, realising the potential political and military value of capturing him alive.

An anchor on a channel affiliated with Iranian state television urged residents in the mountainous region of south-west Iran, where the jet fighter went down, to hand over any "enemy pilot" to police and promised a reward for anyone who did.

That sparked a race over the weekend, with images posted on social media of US aircraft and helicopters flying low over Iran.

![A helicopter launches from the deck of a military ship.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/c7609c5d4c811eda9732fc3e9db78ac8?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Helicopters were deployed as part of the rescue mission. (Supplied: US Navy)

## Black Hawks almost downed

The initial search effort encountered fierce resistance from Iran when it began on Friday, local time, after the F-15 pilot was rescued.

Two Black Hawk helicopters involved in the search were hit by Iranian fire but escaped from Iranian airspace.

The conflict has killed 13 US military service members, with more than 300 wounded, US Central Command says. No US troops have been taken prisoner by Iran.

## What has Iran said?

Although Iran's military claims the US operation was "completely foiled", it has not given a full account of events.

On Sunday evening, an Iranian news agency carried a photo posted by the Revolutionary Guards purporting to show the "skull of an American soldier amid the debris of a destroyed aeroplane".

The guards gave no further information except to add "more proof of the humiliating defeat of the liar Trump".

Has Audio Duration: 7 minutes 16 seconds.

Listen 7m

![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/53d7ee922d89086b17c026981a31ac1c?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=720&cropW=1280&xPos=0&yPos=120&width=862&height=485)

![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/53d7ee922d89086b17c026981a31ac1c?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=720&cropW=1280&xPos=0&yPos=120&width=862&height=485)

Trump unleashes threats after US airman rescued from Iran.

Iranian military spokesman Ebrahim Zolfaghari told state media that US forces had used an abandoned airport in southern Isfahan province, which lies to the north-west of the area where the airman ejected.

He said the aircraft had been taking part in "a deception and escape mission … under the pretext of recovering the pilot of a downed aircraft".

Iranian state media broadcast images of the charred wreckage of what appears to be a plane in a desert area, while officials claimed that two C-130 military transport planes and two Black Hawk helicopters had been destroyed.

In the footage, two charred propellers and engines can be clearly seen, with specialised open-source geolocating experts claiming the images were taken about 50 kilometres south of the city of Isfahan.

Iran's state television on Sunday aired a video showing what it claimed were parts of a US aircraft shot down by Iranian forces, along with a photo of thick, black smoke rising. The broadcaster said Iran had shot down a transport plane and two helicopters that were part of the rescue operation.

The Wall Street Journal and other US media, citing unnamed officials, reported that American forces had blown up two C-130s after they became stuck, in order to prevent them from falling into Iranian hands, with other aircraft flown in to lift rescue teams to safety.

The governor of Kohgiluyeh and Boyer-Ahmad province told Mehr news agency that five people had been killed and seven wounded in the Kuh-e Siah mountain area.

But the governor, Iraj Kazemijou, denied reports that US forces had landed there, saying they were "completely false and have no validity".

**Reuters/AFP/AP**

metadata.title: How US forces pulled off daring rescue of stranded airman shot down in Iran - ABC News

metadata.ogDescription: After an American fighter jet was shot down in a mountainous area of Iran, the US military pulled off an incredible rescue of two airmen, living up to its credo of "no man left behind", despite running into difficulty.
